source_paper_title: Competitive Equilibrium with Indivisible Goods and Generic Budgets
title: Existence of competitive equilibrium for more than two additive agents with almost-equal (generic) budgets
latex:
Given a finite set of indivisible goods \(M=\{1,\dots,m\}\) and \(n>2\) agents \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\), agent \(i\) has an additive valuation \(v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) of the form \(v_i(S)=\sum_{j\in S} v_{ij}\), with \(v_{ij}>0\) (monotone preferences), and budgets \(b_i>0\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^n b_i = 1\). An allocation is a partition \(S=(S_1,\dots,S_n)\) of \(M\). A price vector is \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^m\), and the price of bundle \(T\subseteq M\) is \(p(T)=\sum_{j\in T} p_j\). We say that \(T\) is affordable for agent \(i\) if \(p(T)\le b_i\). A competitive equilibrium (CE) is a pair \((S,p)\) such that for every agent \(i\), the allocated bundle \(S_i\) is demanded at prices \(p\), i.e., \(p(S_i)\le b_i\) and for every bundle \(T\subseteq M\) with \(v_i(T) > v_i(S_i)\) we have \(p(T) > b_i\).

problem: Does there exist \(\varepsilon>0\) (possibly depending only on \(n,m\), or for each valuation profile \((v_i)_{i\in N}\)) such that whenever the budgets are \"sufficiently close to equal\" in the sense that \(\max_{i\in N} \lvert b_i - 1/n\rvert < \varepsilon\) and are not exactly equal (e.g., are generic under an arbitrarily small perturbation), the resulting discrete Fisher market admits a competitive equilibrium \((S,p)\)?
context: Explicitly stated as “problem 1” in Section 1.3.1 immediately after Theorem 1.1, and reiterated in the conclusions (Section 9) as the main open question: extending the almost-equal/generic-budget existence result from 2 agents to \(n>2\) agents.

sources.theorem_or_result:
Exact theorem statements were not accessible from the available web sources.

The paper proves (as stated in the journal abstract) existence results for competitive equilibrium in Fisher markets with indivisible goods under generic (i.e., slightly perturbed) budgets. In particular, for two agents with additive valuations over multiple indivisible goods, making equal budgets generic via an arbitrarily small perturbation guarantees existence of a competitive equilibrium 
- with an integral allocation (a partition of items),
- item prices, and
- each agent receiving a demanded affordable bundle.

The paper also establishes additional existence results for non-equal budgets in special cases of interest (e.g., identical preferences) under generic perturbations of the budget vector.
sources.connection: This is the closest direct precursor to the stated problem, but it operates primarily in the two-agent setting. The results show that arbitrarily small perturbations away from exact equality can restore equilibrium existence, thereby motivating the question of whether a punctured neighborhood around equal budgets guarantees existence. The techniques and counterexamples in this work frame the boundary between exact equal budgets (where nonexistence can occur) and generic, almost-equal budgets (where existence can be recovered in some cases).

sources.theorem_or_result:
Exact theorem statements were not accessible from the available web sources.

Results described in the paper abstract include:
- Nonexistence (for monotone preferences): for each of the cases 
  - 
    \(m=4\) goods and \(n=3\) agents, and
  - 
    \(m=4\) goods and \(n=4\) agents,
  there are monotone (monotonically increasing) preference profiles for which a competitive equilibrium does not exist for almost all income vectors (i.e., except possibly on a measure-zero subset of the income simplex).
- Existence (for additive preferences): when \(m=4\) and \(n=3\) and agents have additive valuations, a competitive equilibrium exists for almost all income vectors.

The paper also provides additional existence/nonexistence characterizations for monotonically decreasing preferences (chores).
sources.connection: The problem asks about \(n>2\) additive agents and whether sufficiently small generic deviations from equal budgets guarantee CE existence. This paper provides the most concrete partial progress for \(n=3\): for \(m=4\) goods and additive valuations, CE exists for almost all incomes, which suggests that equal-budget nonexistence can be fragile in some small markets. At the same time, the negative results for general monotone valuations at \(n\in\{3,4\}, m=4\) show that, beyond additivity, nonexistence can be pervasive even away from equal incomes, indicating that any sought \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood phenomenon is delicate and valuation-class dependent.

sources.theorem_or_result:
Exact theorem statements were not accessible from the available web sources.

As stated in the publication abstract/description, the paper introduces competitive equilibrium from generic incomes (CEGI) and proves existence in the two-agent additive setting when budgets are almost equal (and made generic by perturbation). It also formulates and studies fairness properties for unequal entitlements and establishes additional existence results in special cases (e.g., identical preferences) under generic perturbations of budgets.
sources.connection: This paper is a high-level algorithmic-fair-division presentation of the generic-income approach that directly motivates the posed question. It supplies conceptual and technical framing for why infinitesimal budget inequality can remove degeneracies causing nonexistence at exact equality. Although its core formal guarantees focus on two agents, it articulates the pathway by which one might attempt to extend generic-budget existence to \(n>2\) and highlights which parts remain open.

sources.theorem_or_result:
Exact theorem statements were not accessible from the available web sources.

Main existence result described in the paper abstract:
- For economies with indivisible goods and general (not necessarily additive) preferences, an approximate competitive equilibrium from equal incomes (A-CEEI) exists.
- The approximation allows incomes to be unequal but arbitrarily close (budgets in a small interval around equality) and allows an explicit notion of approximate market clearing (some controlled clearing error or slack), while retaining approximate fairness and approximate efficiency guarantees.
sources.connection: The problem asks for existence of an exact competitive equilibrium under almost-equal but generic budgets. Budish (2011) is the foundational result showing that weakening exact equilibrium (allowing approximate clearing and slight income inequality) yields an existence theorem for arbitrary numbers of agents and goods. This provides both motivation (small budget inequality can help) and a baseline comparison: exact CE existence near equal budgets may fail, yet approximate CE is always achievable under a closely related perturbation paradigm.

sources.theorem_or_result:
Exact theorem statements were not accessible from the available web sources.

Key result described in the abstract:
- Given a Fisher market with additive valuations (whose equilibria may be fractional), it is possible to compute a nearby market obtained by changing agents' budgets by a bounded amount such that the modified market admits an integral (pure) equilibrium in which each good is assigned integrally to some agent.

The paper further leverages this to obtain algorithmic results for computing allocations that combine approximate fairness notions (e.g., relaxations of EF1/proportionality) with Pareto efficiency.
sources.connection: The posed question is fundamentally about whether small perturbations of budgets near equality can restore integral competitive equilibrium existence. This work provides a different, but closely related, budget-perturbation lens: it shows that some budget adjustment (not necessarily arbitrarily small) can enforce integrality of equilibrium outcomes for additive Fisher markets. The techniques suggest that budget perturbation is a powerful degree of freedom, but also indicate that the magnitude of required budget changes may depend on equilibrium prices and can be non-negligible, complicating any uniform \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood existence claim.

sources.theorem_or_result:
Exact theorem statements were not accessible from the available web sources.

Core result described in the abstract:
- The paper develops an "Equilibrium Existence Duality" showing that, with indivisible goods and income effects, competitive equilibrium existence is governed by substitution effects.
- A stated consequence is that net substitutability (a condition weaker than gross substitutability) is sufficient for existence of competitive equilibrium.
- The paper also provides a demand-type classification and conditions characterizing when a pattern of substitution effects guarantees equilibrium existence.
sources.connection: Additive valuations are canonical substitutes, but the presence of hard budgets creates income effects, so classical gross-substitutes Walrasian existence theorems do not directly apply. This paper offers a framework for transporting existence results across settings with and without income effects, which is conceptually relevant to the stated problem. If additive Fisher markets with near-equal budgets could be shown to satisfy the paper’s net-substitutability-type conditions (or an appropriate adaptation), then the duality might yield a route to existence (or explain obstructions) for \(n>2\).

sources.theorem_or_result:
Exact theorem statements were not accessible from the available web sources.

Results described in the abstract:
- The paper proves a Second Welfare Theorem for a broad class of discrete assignment environments (multi-unit demands and general linear constraints) and a strong form of the First Welfare Theorem.
- In particular, it establishes an equivalence between strong efficiency and decentralization through prices in discrete environments (pseudomarkets), showing that strongly efficient outcomes can be implemented via price systems in the model studied.
sources.connection: The stated problem concerns when discrete allocations can be supported by item prices and budgets as a competitive equilibrium. Miralles and Pycia provide general welfare-theoretic conditions under which discrete efficient outcomes are price-implementable in pseudomarkets, a close cousin of discrete Fisher/CEEI-type models used in market design. Although the equilibrium concept and feasibility constraints differ from the exact Fisher budget-feasibility formulation in the problem, their structural equivalence results inform which efficiency notions are compatible with price support and thereby shape potential approaches to establishing or refuting local-existence near equal budgets.

sources.theorem_or_result:
Exact theorem statements were not accessible from the available web sources.

Main results described in the abstract:
- Computing (deciding existence of, or finding) a discrete CEEI allocation is strongly NP-hard.
- The paper introduces a related fairness notion (CEEI-FRAC) that is always Pareto optimal and polynomially verifiable, and shows that computing a CEEI-FRAC discrete assignment is strongly NP-hard in general (with a polynomial-time tractable special case for \(\{0,1\}\)-utilities).
sources.connection: The problem asks an existence question for exact competitive equilibrium under near-equal generic budgets. Even when existence holds, computational barriers can limit constructive approaches. This paper provides baseline hardness results for the equal-income case, emphasizing that existence/verification questions for discrete competitive equilibria are computationally challenging, and suggesting that any \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood existence theorem may not automatically translate into an efficient algorithmic procedure.
